On this page

Cybercrime ​

As more of our lives move online — banking, shopping, communication, health records — the potential for harm grows with it. Cybercrime costs the global economy trillions of rands annually. Understanding the threats, how they work, and how to protect against them is core Paper 2 content.

Grade 11+

Cybercrime types, prevention, and legislation are extended in Grade 11. Network security (encryption, SSL) is a Grade 12 focus.

What is Cybercrime? ​

Cybercrime is any criminal activity that involves a computer or network — either as the tool used to commit the crime, or as the target of the crime.

Types of Cybercrime ​

Hacking ​

Hacking is gaining unauthorised access to a computer system or network.

TypeDescription
Black hat hackerMalicious intent — steal data, cause damage
White hat hackerEthical hacker — paid to find vulnerabilities (penetration testing)
Grey hat hackerBreaks in without permission, but typically reports findings

Methods hackers use:

  • Exploiting software vulnerabilities (bugs/flaws in code)
  • Social engineering (tricking people into revealing credentials)
  • Brute force attacks (trying many passwords until one works)
  • Man-in-the-middle attacks (intercepting communications)

Malware ​

Malware (malicious software) is any program designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a system.

TypeBehaviourHow it spreads
VirusAttaches to a legitimate file; activates when file is openedFile sharing, infected downloads
WormSelf-replicates across networks without user actionNetwork vulnerabilities
Trojan horseDisguised as legitimate software; opens a backdoorMalicious downloads, fake software
RansomwareEncrypts files; demands payment for decryption keyPhishing emails, infected sites
SpywareSecretly monitors user activity, logs keystrokesBundled with free software
AdwareDisplays unwanted advertisementsBundled software, malicious websites
RootkitHides deep in OS, giving persistent hidden accessAdvanced malware
KeyloggerRecords all keystrokes to capture passwordsInstalled by Trojans or physical access
BotnetNetwork of infected "zombie" computers controlled remotelyWorms, Trojans

Phishing ​

Phishing is a social engineering attack that tricks users into revealing sensitive information (passwords, credit card numbers, ID numbers) by pretending to be a trusted entity.

How phishing works:

  1. Victim receives an email appearing to be from their bank/Netflix/SARS
  2. Email contains an urgent message and a link
  3. Link leads to a fake website that looks identical to the real one
  4. Victim enters their credentials — now the attacker has them

Variations:

  • Spear phishing: Targeted phishing using personal information to seem credible
  • Vishing: Voice phishing — phone calls pretending to be from banks/SARS
  • Smishing: SMS phishing — fraudulent text messages
  • Whaling: Phishing targeting senior executives/high-value targets

Identity Theft ​

Identity theft is obtaining and using someone else's personal information — ID number, banking details, passwords — to commit fraud or other crimes.

How information is obtained:

  • Phishing attacks
  • Data breaches
  • Malware (keyloggers, spyware)
  • Shoulder surfing
  • Dumpster diving (finding discarded statements/documents)
  • Social media oversharing

Consequences for victims:

  • Fraudulent bank transactions
  • New accounts or loans opened in their name
  • Criminal records attached to their identity
  • Years to resolve and restore credit

Other Cybercrime Types ​

CrimeDescription
CyberbullyingUsing technology to harass, threaten, or humiliate individuals
Online fraudFake online stores, lottery scams, advance fee fraud (419 scams)
DDoS attackDistributed Denial of Service — flooding a server with requests to crash it
SQL injectionInserting malicious SQL code into a web form to access/manipulate a database
SpoofingFaking the sender identity of an email/message or IP address
PiracyDistributing copyrighted software, music, film without authorisation
Cyber espionageStealing confidential government or corporate data
Sexting / image-based abuseDistributing intimate images without consent

Protecting Against Cybercrime ​

Technical Measures ​

MeasureWhat it does
Antivirus / anti-malwareDetects and removes malicious software
FirewallMonitors and filters incoming/outgoing network traffic
Software updates / patchesFixes known security vulnerabilities
EncryptionScrambles data so only authorised parties can read it
HTTPS / SSLEncrypts web traffic between browser and server
Two-factor authentication (2FA)Requires second verification (OTP, app) beyond password
Strong passwordsLong, complex, unique — not reused across sites
Password managerGenerates and stores unique complex passwords
VPNEncrypts all internet traffic; hides IP address
BackupRegular copies of data so ransomware attacks can be recovered from

Human Measures (Education) ​

PracticeWhy
Never click suspicious links in emailsPrevents phishing
Verify the sender's actual email addressSpoofed display names look real
Never give passwords or OTPs over phoneBanks never ask for this
Use 2FA on important accountsEven compromised passwords need a second factor
Keep software updatedPatches close exploited vulnerabilities
Be cautious of what you share on social mediaReduces spear phishing effectiveness
Use HTTPS sites for any sensitive transactionPrevents man-in-the-middle interception

South African Legislation ​

ActRelevance
Electronic Communications and Transactions Act (ECT Act, 2002)Criminalises hacking, data interception, computer misuse
Cybercrimes Act (19 of 2020)More comprehensive; covers ransomware, unlawful access, fake information
POPIA (Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013)Organisations must protect personal data; failure = fines up to R10 million

Key Terms ​

TermDefinition
CybercrimeCriminal activity involving a computer or network
MalwareSoftware designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access
VirusMalware that attaches to files and spreads when shared
WormSelf-replicating malware that spreads through networks
Trojan horseMalware disguised as legitimate software
RansomwareMalware that encrypts files and demands payment
PhishingDeceptive messages tricking users into revealing credentials
Spear phishingTargeted phishing using personal information
Identity theftUsing someone else's personal information for fraud
DDoSDistributed Denial of Service — overwhelming a server with traffic
SQL injectionInserting malicious SQL code through a web form
FirewallHardware/software filtering network traffic for security
2FATwo-Factor Authentication — two forms of verification required
VPNVirtual Private Network — encrypts and anonymises internet traffic
POPIASouth African law protecting personal information

Exam Focus ​

High-Frequency Questions

  1. "Explain what phishing is and describe how to identify a phishing email" — fake trusted sender, urgent message, suspicious link; identify by: hover over link (URL mismatch), sender email doesn't match organisation, grammar errors, unsolicited request

  2. "Name and describe THREE types of malware" — know virus, worm, Trojan, ransomware, spyware — always describe what each does specifically

  3. "Give FOUR measures to protect a computer against cybercrime" — can mix technical (antivirus, firewall, 2FA, updates) with human (strong passwords, don't click links)

  4. "What is the difference between a virus and a worm?" — virus: attaches to files, needs user action to spread; worm: self-replicates, spreads automatically through networks

  5. "Why is two-factor authentication more secure than a password alone?" — even if password is stolen/guessed, attacker still needs second factor (OTP, physical token) which they don't have
